speaker: interviewer
text: CREW' 1995. Survivor, Flora Mihael Carasso. This is tape number three.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the-- when the Americans liberated you, where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to a village called [INAUDIBLE]. It was a Nazi officers'-- an SS officers' village, where families of SS officers lived. And the American troops [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] took out the families, and they put us in their homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember a German lady sweeping the streets, and the owner of the house begging my mother to give her the blankets, and the mother taking them out of the bed and went to the window and tried to give it to her. And then my father comes and he pulls her back and said, who gave you a blanket for your child all these months? And he didn't let her give the blankets. That's what I remember very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, we were free, of course. The American troops used to pass with cars, with big trucks, Shabbat, and they will throw us chewing gums and chocolates. There was a lot of food. And people died. Many died because they ate too much. And we were out stealing. I mean stealing. It was the first time in my life that I saw my mother steal. She didn't steal things that she needed. She would take a plate. She would take anything she could find. And my father, having-- holding a small hammer in his hand, going down in the cellar, where he found a lot of china, all stolen from different countries in the world, and he would tap them with a hammer and break everything. And he would take [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a wheel of a bicycle-- of a bicycle-- he didn't use it-- a typewriter, anything he could get his hands on with no use for us. But it was like a fee-- the pleasure of revenge, of giving back your feelings to the Germans that you wanted to-- you want to destroy as much as they destroyed you. And everybody calling, come, open the cellar. You see there. And we found many things from Greece, also. The only think I had, it was a dollhouse with toys. For me, it was paradise, because after the war, to have toys to play with. And after a week, the American troops told us that we had to leave. And we put everything in suitcases, and I remember that truck going back. And we went on-- we climbed the truck, but our suitcases stayed on the sidewalk. And we lost even the little things we had of our own. We were stripped of everything. And they took us to Belgium, to a convent. [NON-ENGLISH] It was a convent. And my father used to tell the nuns, [NON-ENGLISH], the nuns pray for us. From there they took us to [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was a center for deportees. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Belgium. I don't know the exact date. And from [NON-ENGLISH] we went to Paris by our-- by-- we went to Paris. It means, we were not taken to Paris. We went because my father had a brother living in Paris who thought that he was lost. He managed to find his brother. He knocked on the door. He wouldn't open. He would say, I'm you're brother, Rene. He said, no, my brother is-- was killed by the Germans. He was so thin, he wouldn't recognize his brother. And we stayed in Paris for about a month or so. Then we went to Italy. And from Italy, we-- we were put on a ship. It was called the Scania-- I remember the name-- in Bali. And we arrived in Athens, where there we were united with my Aunt [INAUDIBLE]. [BACKGROUND NOISE] She was my mother's first cousin, the only survivor-- the survivor. And after a while, we returned back in Salonika, trying to see if any of our relatives had come back, and tried to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] find our home, our furniture, our belongings. We didn't-- my father didn't mention Palestine or United States or staying in Paris. He-- they wanted to go back to Salonika.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even when you were traveling from Paris to Italy, your parents always had the intentions of returning to Salonika?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Intentions of returning, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you return to your-- to your original home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no. We rented a house with my aunt. We just rented a house. And then little by little, selling pieces of jewelry-- because we didn't have any incomes-- my father went to different houses where he knew that they had our furniture. He took one thing and the other-- a table, a chair. And little by little he started-- he-- he wrote to his director and they took him back to his job. And with the first money that he earned, he bought a piano for me, because all the good and polite-- the Jewish girl had to learn how to play the piano. And I have this piano at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Greeks, who were your neighbors before the war, receive you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in another neighborhood. We didn't-- you know, Jews didn't mingle with-- with Greeks-- we are Greeks-- with Grecian-- my mother didn't have any Greek friends. It was always the family. We-- they had friends-- Armenians. But not Greeks. I don't remember any. I remember, afterward, going to school and playing with my classmates and having friends there. But my-- my parents, with her cousin, and with the friends of the Jewish community, the Jewish community started to gather up the-- whatever the other Jews lost. It means that our Jewish community's very rich, because they-- the government gave them what the Jews that were not back lost. That means buildings and assets that they could find. The government had some, of course. But they gave it back to the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they helped many people that had nothing. They helped them. And they sent many young people to Palestine. And there were 5,000 Jews that returned to Salonika, but most of them, because they didn't find any families, they left for Palestine, the United States, or different other countries where they had relatives. How many-- about how many Jews returned to Salonika?

speaker: interviewer
text: And out of that-- out of those 5,000, how many remain today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1,200. And they're not from Salonika, all of them They're from different parts of Greece. Many have died. Many have gone to Athens to live with the children, because the children went to Athens. And so our community is very small. But I think it's a strong community, and we care for each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had private lessons that prepared me to-- the lost-- prepared me to go to school, not to lose any-- not to lose the years that I was at the camp. And I went to school two years in the grammar school. I was very, very, very sensitive about my Jewishness. I-- I was angry if I heard anything about the Jews. I would always come back to my mother and tell her what happened. And fortunately, our community had, from the beginning, started a Jewish children's club, so we were able to meet with other children, to go to summer camps, to have our holidays. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even later in life, many couples were married because we were together and we met. I would never think of meeting or marrying not in the Jewish community in my time. This didn't pass in my mind. I-- our neighbourhood was very Jewish. There were about six or seven families living in the same street. I was very angry with my mother when she didn't speak Greek in public space-- places. I used to pinch her all the time and say, speak Greek, speak Greek, not Spanish or French, because people would turn their heads to look at us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She wouldn't care. She didn't-- she would answer, learn French and Latin as I do. She didn't learn the language, even when my boys were born. They would always tease my parents of the mistakes they made. They were very proud or selfish about their inheritance. They didn't mingle with other people, except the community. They were always feeling that they could tell them something that will bother them and feel sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you went-- finished school, what were your plans then?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I finished my grammar school, or, as we say high school? Because it's a gymnasium. When I went to school, it was six years of grammar school and six years of gymnasium. They wanted to give me the best of education. I had the piano lessons. I had to learn French-- of course, French, always. And they sent me to Anatolia College. It's not a college. It's under the Greek educational system, but it's called Anatolia College. I went there. And after graduating, I wanted to study-- to go to the university and study-- I know the word in French. It's [FRENCH]. It means anything that concerns children. But I had met my husband. We were in love for many years. And I got married. It was not so-- the parents wanted the children to get married, and not so much for higher education.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you and your husband lived close to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived with my parents. We lived with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eno. Eno Mario Mihael. [INAUDIBLE] Eno.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are your children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest son's name is Michel. He is named after two of my husband's uncles that were lost during the the Holocaust. And my younger son is Ari, after my father's name. Well, we changed the name. His fa-- his name was [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] was very Jewish for the-- for our-- for the Greek community. And we called him Ari, because in Greek, Ares is the god of war. And even me, I call him Ari, and all of the family. But his classmates and all his Greek friends call him Ari. That was very fine. They would call him Ari. And we would call him Ari. And after he went-- he went to study in Israel-- suddenly he changed his name to Ariel. He's-- he's renamed, I think. He knows better. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you used to tell your children about your experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both my husband and me. I don't think that my parents told them many things. But my husband and me, every single minute that we had the opportunity. Everything. We were not that kind of parents that would say, don't say this to the children because they will be sorry, because we don't want to tell our experiences because we will be sorry. No. No. They must not forget. We told everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever have the fear that a Holocaust could happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same things-- the things that happen to Jews? Especially, only-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, I don't think now that there's a state of Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But you will see what is happening in the world-- in the world with-- in other nations as the Kurds-- how you call them now? That the Turks are-- It's like a Holocaust. It's like a genocide also. But I'm not afraid. I know that, always, if-- they can't chase me from Greece. And if I want to go someplace, I have where to go. I don't think about it. No, it doesn't frighten me at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you were active now in an organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What organization is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's WIZO. It's Women's International Zionist Organization. It's an organization that is spread all over the world, in 55 countries. And it's always volunteer worker. We have a lot of-- about [? 1700 ?] institutions in Israel-- baby care centers, creche, and clubs for youth, elderly homes. And we try to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to do our best and our share to keep these institutes that help the-- help the Israelis to go on with their lives and to be better citizens. It's a little bit difficult in Greece, but we try. We believe in our work. And we do-- I can say, women do miracles. And the best is now that we will have, in the name of [INAUDIBLE] in Salonika, a building donated by my aunt. All her assets were donated to WIZO, and there will be a very large building with 64 offices. And I had the honor of knowing that my husband's name will be there on a plaque.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would it be possible now to invite your son to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we meet your son now? Meet your son, Ariel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Mihael, want to introduce us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a my younger son, Ariel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His brother lives in Salonika. He was married last August. And now he's going to be a father in two months. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. My brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not my-- [LAUGHS] my older son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were raised in Greece?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Salonika.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother take you back to show you her original house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just where they used to live before I was born. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know-- where they sleep during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you very interested in your mother's and father's experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: More-- more about my father experience, because it was more exciting, because they were hiding in Athens. My mother's was more of a mystery. And they didn't say much, even though she said that they did. But my father was hiding in Athens, so it was more-- more of intrigue.

speaker: interviewer
text: He hid as a-- a non-Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was an alter boy in Athens.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And all his family survived. There were four-- four kids, and they all survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much, both of you for joining us.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you for helping to keep, and perpetuate, everything that happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope my children will keep on remembering when I won't be around. And they will remember, and tell everything to their children. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is a photo of me. I think I'm two years old. It must be at the end of 1937. I'm with my mother. And I'm-- now I'm looking to the balloon she's holding. And I remember, even as a young child, asking always for a balloon. It was my greatest pleasure to have a balloon. And the other girl is my nanny. I don't remember her name, of course. And these photos were taken in Salonika. And I can remember the name of the photographer. His name was [INAUDIBLE]. He was the best in our city. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This is a photo of me, a baby, in my baby carriage with my cousin, Alberto Bezelie. He was about five years older than me. A very handsome boy, a very good boy. And he was my best friend and protector always. He ran after me, protecting me, from my mother, because she was very strict and very negative, always negative with me. And he was always near me and protected me. Unfortunately, he didn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly. Going for a promenade. I don't know. Because our town has changed so much, you wouldn't recognize it. You can't find your own-- old houses. [INAUDIBLE] they're all new buildings, and everything is new, now. He was always dressed with this Tyrolean suit. I remember that. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is my Spanish passport until the year 1956, one year before my marriage. Then, marrying my husband, who had the Greek nationality, I renounced mine, and I got his nationality. And I have a Greek passport now.

speaker: interviewer
text: That is the one that saved you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, this is the passport that saved us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it say?

speaker: interviewee
text: It says that we can travel everywhere except to the satellite countries. It meant the Communist countries, I think. And motivo-- and the why I was traveling was because of family reasons. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This is my husband's false identity card, which he always carried during the years he was hiding in Athens, in a region called [INAUDIBLE]. And his first name was [? Mixauyidus ?] [? Nikolaus. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after the war, many-- his brother used to call him Nick, even though his name was Eno, because his false name during the war was Nicholas. And he used to call him Nick. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is the-- a photo taken on the-- on April 13, 1945. And you can see the train where-- when the Germans deserted us and left us free near a forest. I don't know exactly what place this is. And this is the place where we were liberated by the American army. We tried to cook whatever we found, especially potatoes and beets. I remember big beets. You see the smoke at the far end. It's smoke from the fires that women tried to light to boil some potatoes. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in our synagogue. It must've been Rosh Hashanah. And the seventh from the left is my mother-in-law, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Serena Mihael. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture of my mother, who sits on the far end-- the right end. There's my mother. Next to me is my Aunt Margot. And I am the one with the [INAUDIBLE] there. And we have coffee on the terrace of a restaurant overlooking over our city. It's of course after the war. It must be 1966 or '67.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother's to the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's to the right, yes, in the blue dress. In the yellow, there's my aunt. And then I'm the pink, there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in our club. We have a special big room for WIZO.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's Hanukkah, where we-- all the ladies that had a special happy event during the year light a candle to honor marriages, graduations, births. And I'm talking and speaking about the holiday. And that's why you see so many [? chanukiah ?] there. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Can I say something else?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you did that a little bit down the picture-- no, no. Past the [? chanukiah. ?] I'll show you this-- there on the side. You see, lower, here. [POINTS] You see this couple there? Yeah, there. That's my Aunt Margot, who you saw before, and her husband, Henri, who donated their fortune. And there is the building that will be built, that's already started building, in Tel Aviv for WIZO. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They're our biggest donors. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is the picture taken the day of the wedding of my son, Michel, on August 27th, 1994. And there is Michel, his bride, and daughter-in-law Shelly, and my son, Ariel. It was one of my happiest days, but unfortunately, my husband's not there. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] That's a picture that Ariel took, I'm sure. It's my husband, Eno, and me. He was a wonderful person. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Unfortunately, he died one year ago and two months. He was a wonderful husband, and especially father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I thank him for the good children he gave me. [SOBBING]
